writing_task: Hey, I want to document the token refresh flow itself — specifically what happens when `TokenSource.Token()` is called and the token is expired or about to expire. I need it to cover the internal state machine (checking expiry, deciding whether to refresh, handling concurrent refresh requests) and how errors from the upstream auth service propagate back to the caller. Same audience as before: competent engineers who are new to this system.

drafts.generic:
When Token() is called, the source first checks whether the current token remains valid beyond a short expiry buffer, typically a few seconds before actual expiration. If the token is still fresh, it returns immediately.

If the token is expired or within that buffer, the source attempts a refresh. To avoid redundant upstream calls under concurrent load, only one goroutine proceeds with the refresh while others wait on a shared channel or sync primitive. Once the refresh completes, all waiters receive the result.

If the upstream auth service returns an error, that error propagates directly to every waiting caller. No stale token is returned as a fallback.

user_reason: Draft A is the only one doing the work I actually care about. The explanation of why single-flight over a plain mutex — including what the mutex approach would have done wrong and why we accepted the stall tradeoff — is exactly the kind of rejected-alternative reasoning that teaches someone the system rather than just describing it. It's not finished, but what's there is strong. Draft B is decent: the code example is minimal and includes the error wrapping pattern, and the note about errors not being cached is useful context. But the singleflight explanation just says what it does without saying why that over alternatives, and the 'One edge case worth knowing' placement is backwards — that warning should appear before the section where someone would encounter it, not as an afterthought. Draft C is too thin to be useful — 'shared channel or sync primitive' is vague hand-waving that leaves a new engineer no better informed than before they read it, and there's no reasoning anywhere about why any decision was made.

criteria_analysis.name: Design Decision Reasoning
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft excels here: it names rejected alternatives explicitly (mutex-per-refresh, read-write lock, wrapping errors, evicting on failure) and states the tradeoffs of each chosen approach, giving the reader enough context to evaluate future changes. The generic draft names no alternatives and provides no reasoning. The original preference draft mentions singleflight but never names what it replaced or why, and doesn't discuss tradeoffs of the chosen approach.

criteria_analysis.name: Code Example Discipline
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft has two code blocks, each with a heading comment stating what it accomplishes ('Fast path: return cached token...', 'Slow path: deduplicate concurrent refresh requests...'), both well under 15 lines, with prose doing the heavy lifting. The generic draft has no code at all, which trivially satisfies the discipline constraint (no violations). The original preference draft has a code snippet with no heading comment describing what it accomplishes, and the snippet carries conceptual weight (the singleflight pattern) that isn't fully explained in prose beforehand.

criteria_analysis.name: Prose-Code Weight Distribution
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft front-loads the conceptual model extensively — the two-phase pattern, mutex strategy, preconditions, and failure modes are all explained before the code appears, and the code itself carries minimal conceptual weight. It explicitly calls out the precondition about lock ordering before the code block. The generic draft has no code so distribution is moot but the prose alone doesn't build a deep conceptual model. The original preference draft introduces code before fully explaining the surrounding design choices, and the singleflight pattern appears in code before the mutex/locking strategy is discussed.

criteria_analysis.name: Recommendation Framing
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft consistently names alternative patterns, acknowledges they work ('Under a plain mutex, later callers could at least proceed independently'), and explains concretely why they lose in this context. The generic draft makes no recommendations and names no alternatives. The original preference draft implicitly recommends singleflight and not caching errors, but never names the alternative patterns or acknowledges their merits.

suggested_rubric.name: Design Decision Reasoning
suggested_rubric.description: Every documented design choice names the rejected alternative explicitly and explains why it was worse, using the 'we chose X over Y because Z' framing. The reasoning gives the reader enough context to evaluate proposed changes, not just understand current behavior — this is what they can't get from reading the code.

dimensions.label: Each design choice names at least one specific rejected alternative
Tradeoffs of the chosen approach are stated explicitly, not just its benefits
Explanations convey architectural reasoning that cannot be derived from reading the implementation
The reasoning gives the reader enough context to evaluate whether a future change would be appropriate

suggested_rubric.name: Code Example Discipline
suggested_rubric.description: Each code example has a one-line heading comment stating what it accomplishes (not how), stays at or under ~15 lines so the reader can hold the whole function in their head at once, and avoids carrying conceptual weight that belongs in prose. When an example is too long, the fix is moving explanation to surrounding prose, not cutting logic from the code.

dimensions.label: Every code example has a single-line comment at the top describing what it accomplishes
The heading comment lets the reader determine whether this snippet is relevant before reading any code
Functions and code blocks are approximately 15 lines or fewer
When a code block is long, the fix is moving explanation to prose rather than cutting logic

suggested_rubric.name: Prose-Code Weight Distribution
suggested_rubric.description: Explanatory prose before code examples front-loads the conceptual model, structural choices, and failure modes so the code only needs to show the shape of the pattern. If something is explained by an inline comment, the prose hasn't done its job yet. Warnings and preconditions appear before the section where someone would encounter the problem, not after.

dimensions.label: Why the code is shaped a particular way (e.g., mutex strategy, helper functions, branching) is explained in prose before the code block
Every notable construct in the code (helpers, naming distinctions, two-phase patterns) is introduced in the preceding prose
Warnings, preconditions, and concurrency requirements appear before the code block they apply to, not after
Inline code comments do not restate what the surrounding prose has already explained

suggested_rubric.name: Recommendation Framing
suggested_rubric.description: When recommending an approach, the doc names the alternative pattern, acknowledges that it works, and then explains concretely why it's the worse default. Directives become reasoning the reader internalizes rather than instructions they follow blindly.

dimensions.label: The recommended approach is compared to at least one named alternative pattern
The alternative is acknowledged as functional, not dismissed outright
The specific weakness of the alternative is explained concretely
The reader finishes knowing why to use the recommendation, not just that they should

suggested_rubric.name: Narrative Coherence
suggested_rubric.description: Section headers and reading order tell a coherent story from setup through integration through troubleshooting — the doc flows like a tutorial even when it's reference material. Later sections reference earlier design decisions via callbacks rather than re-explanation. Language is precise and respects the reader's competence.

dimensions.label: Documents long enough to require navigation use section headers that convey a narrative progression — continuous prose without headers fails this when the reader cannot scan for a specific phase
Later sections reference earlier design reasoning with explicit callbacks rather than re-explaining
When a concept depends on another section, the dependency is named with a direct reference
Language is precise and avoids minimizing words ('simply', 'just', 'easy') that assume difficulty away

writing_task: Hey, now that we've got the token refresh flow documented, I want to cover what happens when refresh actually fails — like network errors, invalid credentials, or the refresh token itself being expired. I need something that explains how errors propagate back to the caller, what the caller is expected to do in each case, and what internal state the system is left in after a failure. Can you draft a section on that?

drafts.generic: When a token refresh attempt fails, the type of failure determines both the error returned to the caller and the resulting internal state. A network error is treated as transient: the system returns a retriable error code, leaves the existing token state unchanged, and expects the caller to retry after an appropriate backoff. If the refresh token has expired or is otherwise invalid, the system returns a non-retriable authentication error, clears all stored token state, and expects the caller to redirect the user through a full reauthentication flow. Invalid credentials produce the same non-retriable error and state clearing. In all failure cases, no partial token state is written, ensuring the system remains consistent.

user_reason: C is doing the most important thing right: it explains the architectural decision by naming what was rejected and why — the typed enum vs generic error string section is exactly the kind of reasoning that teaches someone something. It also front-loads the warning about conflating failure categories before the caller would make that mistake, which is the right placement. A is solid — it has structure, the code examples look minimal and contextual, and it separates the three cases clearly. The incomplete ending on the expired refresh token section hurts it but it's recoverable. B is a wall of prose with no headers, no code, and no rejected alternatives — it restates what the system does without teaching anything. It reads like a spec comment, not documentation.

The original preference draft describes the three failure modes but never names rejected alternatives or explains why the system was designed this way rather than another way.

writing_task: Hey, I want to document the token storage layer next — specifically how we handle the in-memory cache versus the persistent fallback, and when each gets written to or invalidated. Can you draft a section that covers the read path first (how a token gets retrieved before we ever hit the network), then the write path (what triggers a cache update), and finally the invalidation conditions? Assume the reader already understands the refresh flow from the previous doc.

drafts.generic:
The read path begins with the in-memory cache. When a token is requested, the system checks the in-memory store first. If a valid, non-expired token exists there, it is returned immediately without touching the network or persistent storage. If the in-memory cache is empty or holds an expired entry, the system falls back to persistent storage, reads the token from there, repopulates the in-memory cache, and returns the result.

On the write path, the cache updates whenever a token is newly issued or successfully refreshed. Both the in-memory store and persistent storage are written to together in that moment.

Invalidation occurs under three conditions: explicit logout, a detected token expiry during a read attempt, or an upstream authentication error indicating the token has been revoked. Any of these clears both layers simultaneously, ensuring neither stale in-memory data nor outdated persistent entries survive.
drafts.preference:
Token Storage Layer

Read Path: Cache Before Network

When a token is requested, the system checks the in-memory cache first. If a valid, non-expired entry exists, it returns immediately — no I/O, no network call.

```
token = cache.get(key)
if token and not token.is_expired():
    return token
return persistent_store.get(key)
```

If the cache misses, the persistent store serves as fallback. A hit there repopulates the in-memory cache before returning.

Write Path: What Triggers Updates

Both layers write on token acquisition and successful refresh. The persistent store also writes on process shutdown to survive restarts.

Invalidation Conditions

Tokens are invalidated on expiry, explicit revocation signals, or scope changes. Invalidation clears both layers together — partial invalidation is not supported.

user_reason: Draft A is clearly the best — it leads with the failure mode warning before the section where someone would encounter it, explains the rwmutex choice by explicitly naming what was rejected (channel-based lock) and why, and the code example is minimal and has a one-line 'what this does' comment. The story is starting to build context properly. It's cut off mid-sentence which is a problem, but the approach is right. Draft C at least has headers that gesture toward a narrative structure and keeps code minimal, but the pseudocode example lacks the 'what this does' comment, there's no explanation of rejected alternatives anywhere, and the warnings about partial invalidation come after the invalidation section rather than before. Draft B is the weakest — it's a wall of prose with no structure, no code, no rejected alternatives explained, and reads like a summary someone wrote from memory rather than documentation that teaches anything. writing_task: Hey, now that the storage layer is documented I want to move on to the cache invalidation logic — specifically the rules around TTL expiration versus explicit invalidation (like when a token gets revoked mid-session), and how those two paths interact. Can you draft a section on that? Same structure as before — headers that tell a story, decisions explained with what we rejected, and keep any code examples tight.

drafts.generic:
Cache Invalidation: TTL Expiration and Explicit Revocation

Two invalidation paths exist and they are not equal. TTL expiration is passive — the cache entry ages out naturally, and no external signal is required. Explicit revocation is active — a token is invalidated mid-session, and the cache must respond immediately regardless of remaining TTL.

We rejected a single unified path where revocation simply shortened the TTL. That approach introduced race conditions during high-revocation events and made audit trails ambiguous.

Instead, revocation writes a tombstone key that read operations check first. If a tombstone exists, the entry is treated as expired regardless of TTL. Tombstones themselves expire after the maximum possible TTL, keeping storage clean without manual cleanup.

user_reason: C is the strongest by a clear margin. The code example is minimal, runnable, and has the one-line 'what this does' comment I want — and critically it shows the tombstone-first ordering inline rather than just asserting it in prose. The rejected alternative (setting TTL to zero) is explained with the actual reason it was rejected: coupling between the auth revocation path and the cache layer. That's the kind of context that teaches. A is doing the right things conceptually — it explains rejected alternatives, it's honest about tradeoffs, the prose flows — but it's cut off mid-section which is an obvious problem, and 'worth stating plainly' is a little self-congratulatory. Still, what's there is better than B. B is the weakest: it mentions a rejected alternative but gives almost no depth on why ('race conditions during high-revocation events' is vague, 'audit trails ambiguous' is unexplained), there's no code example at all, and the tombstone mechanism is described without showing how it actually works. It reads like a summary of a document rather than the document itself.

suggested_rubric.name: Design Decision Reasoning
suggested_rubric.description: Every documented design choice names the rejected alternative explicitly and explains why it was worse, using the 'we chose X over Y because Z' framing. The rejection reasoning must name the concrete mechanism, property, or coupling that made the alternative worse — not just that it was worse or that it introduced vague problems. The reasoning gives the reader enough context to evaluate proposed changes, not just understand current behavior — this is what they can't get from reading the code.

dimensions.label: Each design choice names at least one specific rejected alternative
Tradeoffs of the chosen approach are stated explicitly, not just its benefits
Explanations convey architectural reasoning that cannot be derived from reading the implementation
The reasoning gives the reader enough context to evaluate whether a future change would be appropriate
The rejection reasoning names the specific mechanism, coupling, or property that made the alternative worse — not just that it introduced problems or race conditions

suggested_rubric.name: Code Example Discipline
suggested_rubric.description: Each code example has a one-line heading comment stating what it accomplishes (not how), stays at or under ~15 lines so the reader can hold the whole function in their head at once, and is self-contained enough to be intelligible without mentally gap-filling undefined references. When an example requires too much scaffolding to be intelligible, the fix is narrowing the slice to show only the caller-facing pattern, not adding more context. When the point being made is an ordering constraint or behavioral invariant, the code example should demonstrate it directly — not just assert it in prose.

dimensions.label: Every code example has a single-line comment at the top describing what it accomplishes
The heading comment lets the reader determine whether this snippet is relevant before reading any code
Functions and code blocks are approximately 15 lines or fewer
The example does not reference undefined external functions, types, or helpers that force the reader to mentally gap-fill; when too much scaffolding would be needed, the slice is narrowed instead
When a code block is long, the fix is moving explanation to prose rather than cutting logic
When the point being made is an ordering constraint or behavioral invariant (e.g., tombstone checked before TTL), the code demonstrates it directly rather than relying solely on prose assertion

suggested_rubric.name: Prose-Code Weight Distribution
suggested_rubric.description: Explanatory prose before code examples front-loads the conceptual model, structural choices, and failure modes so the code only needs to show the shape of the pattern. If something is explained by an inline comment, the prose hasn't done its job yet. Warnings and preconditions appear before the section where someone would encounter the problem, not after. When the code's structure itself is the argument — for example, the ordering of checks encodes a security invariant — prose should frame the question the code answers, not pre-explain the answer the reader should see in the code.

dimensions.label: Why the code is shaped a particular way (e.g., mutex strategy, helper functions, branching) is explained in prose before the code block
Every notable construct in the code (helpers, naming distinctions, two-phase patterns) is introduced in the preceding prose
Warnings, preconditions, and concurrency requirements appear before the code block they apply to, not after
Inline code comments do not restate what the surrounding prose has already explained

When code structure carries the argument (e.g., check ordering), prose sets up the question the code answers rather than pre-explaining the answer — letting the code teach rather than merely illustrate

suggested_rubric.name: Narrative Coherence
suggested_rubric.description: Section headers and reading order tell a coherent story — the doc flows like a guided walkthrough even when it's reference material. Headers describe what is happening at each stage, not just label the mechanism. When two sections are conceptually entangled, the one with more dangerous failure modes or that serves as a prerequisite for correct reasoning comes first.

dimensions.label: Section headers describe what is happening at each stage rather than labeling the mechanism — e.g., 'Checking Whether the Cached Token Is Still Usable' rather than 'Expiry Check'
Content that walks through a sequence of stages is broken into distinct subsections with headers, not presented as a single long block
When two sections are entangled, the one the reader needs to reason correctly about subsequent material comes first — especially state information, pitfalls, or caveats that affect interpretation
Later sections reference earlier design reasoning with explicit callbacks rather than re-explaining
When a concept depends on another section, the dependency is named with a direct reference

suggested_rubric.name: Recommendation Framing
suggested_rubric.description: When recommending an approach, the doc names the alternative pattern, acknowledges that it works, and then explains concretely why it's the worse default. Directives become reasoning the reader internalizes rather than instructions they follow blindly.

dimensions.label: The recommended approach is compared to at least one named alternative pattern
The alternative is acknowledged as functional, not dismissed outright
The specific weakness of the alternative is explained concretely
The reader finishes knowing why to use the recommendation, not just that they should

writing_task: Hey, I want to document the token refresh flow next — specifically how the auth service decides when to issue a new access token versus rejecting the refresh request entirely. There's some non-obvious logic around refresh token rotation and what happens when a refresh token gets used more than once (which can indicate token theft). Can you put together a draft that covers the decision logic and the error paths?

drafts.rubric:
Token Refresh Flow: How the Auth Service Decides Whether to Issue or Reject

When a client presents a refresh token, the service does not treat validation as a single yes/no check. It runs three ordered checks — existence, tombstone status, and expiry — and the ordering is not arbitrary. Read the tombstone check first, because a tombstoned token must be rejected even if it has not expired, and understanding why determines how you should interpret every error path that follows.

We chose refresh token rotation (each use produces a new refresh token and immediately invalidates the old one) over long-lived static refresh tokens because static tokens give an attacker an indefinitely usable credential once stolen, with no signal to the server that theft occurred. The tradeoff is that rotation adds a failure mode that static tokens do not have: a legitimate client that retries a network-failed request will attempt to re-use a token the server already rotated, and the server cannot distinguish that from an attacker replaying the old token. Static tokens would sidestep that ambiguity entirely — so if your deployment has highly unreliable networks and very low theft risk, static tokens are not unreasonable; they are just the worse default for most threat models.

The reuse-detection mechanism works through tombstones. When a refresh token is used, the service stores a tombstone record keyed to that token's ID before issuing the successor. On any subsequent request carrying that token ID, the service checks for the tombstone before checking expiry. The question the following code answers is: which check comes first, and what happens to the token family when the wrong order would silently lose security coverage.

    # Reuse detection: tombstone must be checked before TTL
    def validate_refresh_token(token_id, token_family_id):
        if tombstone_store.exists(token_id):
            revoke_entire_family(token_family_id)
            raise TokenReuseDetectedError(token_id)
        if not token_store.exists(token_id):
            raise TokenNotFoundError(token_id)
        token = token_store.get(token_id)
        if token.expires_at < now():
            raise TokenExpiredError(token_id)
        return token

Checking TTL before tombstone would cause a race: an expired tombstoned token would fall into the TokenExpiredError path, which does not trigger family revocation. The attacker's copy and the legitimate client's copy would both be rejected without the wider session being invalidated.

Family revocation is the response to detected reuse. We chose full family revocation over single-token invalidation because single-token invalidation stops only the replayed token — it leaves every other token in the rotation chain usable, which preserves the attacker's access if they have already advanced the chain past the replayed token. The concrete mechanism that makes single-token invalidation insufficient is that the attacker, once they have the old token, can race the legitimate client and rotate it first; the server then sees the legitimate client's replay, invalidates only that token, and the attacker's newer token survives untouched. Full family revocation removes that race entirely, at the cost of forcing the user to re-authenticate — which is a real UX penalty worth acknowledging.

The three rejection error types have distinct meanings for callers. TokenReuseDetectedError signals that the entire session is gone and the user must log in again; retrying will not help. TokenNotFoundError means the token ID was never valid or has already been purged from storage after expiry cleanup; callers should treat this identically to reuse from a security standpoint, even though it is usually benign. TokenExpiredError is the only case where the session state is not suspicious — the token simply aged out, and the user needs to re-authenticate through normal login. Callers should not attempt to distinguish TokenReuseDetectedError from TokenNotFoundError in their error handling, because doing so would let an attacker use a probing strategy to determine whether a stolen token has already been used.
drafts.generic:
When a client submits a refresh token, the auth service first checks whether the token exists in the store and has not expired. If valid and unused, the service issues a new access token, immediately invalidates the submitted refresh token, and stores a newly generated refresh token in its place. This is refresh token rotation.

If the submitted token has already been used, the service treats this as a potential theft signal. It immediately invalidates the entire token family — all refresh tokens sharing that lineage — forcing the user to reauthenticate from scratch. The response returns a 401, and the client receives no new credentials under any circumstance.
drafts.preference:
The auth service uses refresh token rotation, meaning each time a client exchanges a refresh token for a new access token, the old refresh token is invalidated and a new one is issued alongside the access token. This keeps the refresh token family short-lived in practice even if individual tokens carry a long expiry.

The critical edge case is reuse detection. The service tracks each refresh token's state: unused, used, or revoked. If a token arrives in a used state, the service assumes theft — it revokes the entire token family immediately and returns a 401, forcing full re-authentication.

```
if token.state == "used":
    revoke_family(token.family_id)
    raise AuthError(401, "Refresh token reuse detected")

if token.state == "revoked":
    raise AuthError(401, "Refresh token revoked")

token.state = "used"
new_access_token = issue_access_token(token.user_id)
new_refresh_token = issue_refresh_token(token.family_id)
return new_access_token, new_refresh_token
```

Expiry is checked before state. An expired token returns a 401 with a distinct error code so clients know to redirect to login rather than retry. A structurally invalid token — bad signature, malformed payload — returns a 400.

user_reason: C is the closest to what I actually want — it has a runnable code example with clear logic, separates the decision paths, and the code does its job in under 15 lines. The missing piece is that it doesn't explain what was rejected and why (why rotation over static tokens?), and 'Expiry is checked before state' comes after the code when it should come before as a warning that shapes how you read the logic. B gets credit for actually explaining the rejected alternative (static tokens) and the tradeoff reasoning — that's the most genuinely useful part of the whole set — but it cuts off mid-sentence and buries the reader in prose before showing anything concrete. The 'read the tombstone check first' instruction is also asking the reader to reorder the document in their head, which means the document isn't ordered correctly. A is the weakest: it's clean prose but it's just restating what the code does with no rejected alternatives, no code example, and no warning before the reuse-detection section about the retry ambiguity that a legitimate client will hit. Someone will implement against A and get surprised by exactly the failure modes it doesn't mention.

criteria_analysis.name: Design Decision Reasoning
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft explicitly names rejected alternatives (static tokens over rotation, single-token invalidation over family revocation) with specific mechanisms explaining why each is worse, states tradeoffs of the chosen approach (UX penalty, network retry failure mode), and equips the reader to evaluate future changes. The generic draft names no alternatives and provides no reasoning beyond describing current behavior. The original preference draft describes the system but never names a rejected alternative or explains why the chosen design was selected over another.

criteria_analysis.name: Code Example Discipline
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft has a heading comment ('Reuse detection: tombstone must be checked before TTL'), stays under ~15 lines, and directly demonstrates the ordering invariant in code. It does reference tombstone_store and token_store which are undefined but reasonably inferrable. The generic draft has no code at all. The original preference draft includes a code example that demonstrates the state-check logic but references undefined helpers (issue_access_token, issue_refresh_token) and lacks a heading comment; it also contradicts itself by saying 'expiry is checked before state' in prose, which undermines the security invariant.

criteria_analysis.name: Prose-Code Weight Distribution
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft front-loads the conceptual model (tombstone mechanism, why ordering matters) and frames the question the code answers ('which check comes first, and what happens to the token family when the wrong order would silently lose security coverage') before the code block, letting the code teach. The single inline comment restates purpose without redundancy. The generic draft has no code so this criterion barely applies, and its prose is too thin to scaffold anything. The original preference draft puts important structural information ('expiry is checked before state') after the code rather than before, and the prose doesn't adequately frame the code's structure.

criteria_analysis.name: Narrative Coherence
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft has a descriptive header ('Token Refresh Flow: How the Auth Service Decides Whether to Issue or Reject') and orders content so the most dangerous failure mode (tombstone ordering) is explained before error paths, matching the rubric's requirement that dangerous prerequisites come first. It lacks subsection headers for the sequential stages, which would improve scannability. The generic draft is too short to have meaningful narrative structure. The original preference draft has a logical flow but doesn't use subsection headers and buries the critical ordering information at the end.

criteria_analysis.name: Recommendation Framing
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft names alternatives (static tokens, single-token invalidation), acknowledges them as functional ('static tokens are not unreasonable'), and explains concretely why they lose ('single-token invalidation stops only the replayed token — it leaves every other token in the rotation chain usable'). The reader finishes understanding why, not just what. The generic draft makes no comparisons and offers no reasoning. The original preference draft describes the current approach without naming or acknowledging any alternative pattern.

per_criterion.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft explicitly names rejected alternatives (static tokens over rotation, single-token invalidation over family revocation) with specific mechanisms explaining why each is worse, states tradeoffs of the chosen approach (UX penalty, network retry failure mode), and equips the reader to evaluate future changes. The generic draft names no alternatives and provides no reasoning beyond describing current behavior. The original preference draft describes the system but never names a rejected alternative or explains why the chosen design was selected over another.

rubric_judge_result.overall_reasoning: The rubric-guided draft excels across all criteria — it names rejected alternatives with specific mechanisms, front-loads architectural reasoning before code, and frames recommendations so the reader internalizes the reasoning rather than following directives. The original preference draft provides a functional description with a code example but lacks the design-decision reasoning and proper prose-code scaffolding the user's rubric demands. The generic draft is too brief to satisfy any criterion meaningfully.

per_criterion.reasoning: Draft A is exceptionally clear, walking through each decision with explicit rationale and naming the specific failure modes that motivate design choices. Draft B is clear but at a high level — it omits the critical ordering logic and the reasoning behind family revocation.

Draft A builds a tight logical chain: rotation rationale → tombstone mechanism → check ordering → family revocation justification → error semantics. Draft B is coherent but brief; the two paragraphs connect logically but leave significant reasoning gaps.
Both drafts are grammatically sound with no errors or typos.

Draft A uses a clear progression from design rationale to code to error taxonomy, with a code block serving as an anchor. Draft B is organized adequately but consists of only two short paragraphs with no structural markers, making it feel more like a summary than a structured explanation.

Draft A is compelling because it surfaces non-obvious tradeoffs and attacker strategies, giving the reader genuine insight. Draft B conveys facts competently but does not engage the reader with the deeper 'why' behind the design.

Draft A maintains a confident, direct technical voice that acknowledges tradeoffs honestly (e.g., 'a real UX penalty worth acknowledging'). Draft B has an appropriate tone but is more neutral and impersonal, offering less authorial perspective.

generic_judge_result.overall_reasoning: Draft A is substantially richer in explanatory depth, design justification, and security reasoning while remaining clearly written and well-organized. Draft B is correct and competent but reads as a brief summary rather than a thorough explanation, leaving out the most valuable insights (check ordering, race conditions, error-handling guidance).

suggestion_text:
- **Design Decision Reasoning**: Kept unchanged — this criterion is clearly working: the rubric-guided draft scored 5 and the original preference draft scored 2, meaning the rubric successfully drove the behavior the user values most (naming rejected alternatives with specific mechanisms). The user's complaint about the original preference draft ('doesn't explain what was rejected and why') confirms this criterion is calibrated correctly.
- **Code Example Discipline**: Kept unchanged — the rubric-guided draft scored 4 and the original preference draft scored 3, and the user ranked the original preference draft first specifically because its code example was 'runnable' and 'does its job in under 15 lines.' The gap isn't in this criterion's definition — the original preference draft's code was closer to what the user wanted for different reasons (concreteness, runnability) that are already covered by existing dimensions like self_contained_slice and invariants_shown_in_code.
- **Prose-Code Weight Distribution**: Reworded the description and added a dimension to surface the specific failure the user identified: critical ordering information (like 'expiry is checked before state') appearing after the code block rather than before it. The user explicitly flagged this as the 'missing piece' in the original preference draft, and the existing preconditions_before_code dimension doesn't fully capture that ordering information which shapes how the reader interprets the code logic must come before the code, not just warnings and preconditions.
- **Narrative Coherence**: Kept unchanged — the rubric-guided draft scored 4 versus the original preference draft's 3, so the criterion is directionally correct. The user's complaint ('read the tombstone check first is asking the reader to reorder the document in their head') is already addressed by the conceptual_dependencies_ordered dimension, which requires dangerous prerequisites to come first.

suggested_rubric.name: Design Decision Reasoning
suggested_rubric.description: Every documented design choice names the rejected alternative explicitly and explains why it was worse, using the 'we chose X over Y because Z' framing. The rejection reasoning must name the concrete mechanism, property, or coupling that made the alternative worse — not just that it was worse or that it introduced vague problems. The reasoning gives the reader enough context to evaluate proposed changes, not just understand current behavior — this is what they can't get from reading the code.

dimensions.label: Each design choice names at least one specific rejected alternative
Tradeoffs of the chosen approach are stated explicitly, not just its benefits
Explanations convey architectural reasoning that cannot be derived from reading the implementation
The reasoning gives the reader enough context to evaluate whether a future change would be appropriate
The rejection reasoning names the specific mechanism, coupling, or property that made the alternative worse — not just that it introduced problems

suggested_rubric.name: Code Example Discipline
suggested_rubric.description: Each code example has a one-line heading comment stating what it accomplishes (not how), stays at or under ~15 lines so the reader can hold the whole function in their head at once, and is self-contained enough to be intelligible without mentally gap-filling undefined references. When an example requires too much scaffolding to be intelligible, the fix is narrowing the slice to show only the caller-facing pattern, not adding more context. When the point being made is an ordering constraint or behavioral invariant, the code example should demonstrate it directly.

dimensions.label: Every code example has a single-line comment at the top describing what it accomplishes
The heading comment lets the reader determine whether this snippet is relevant before reading any code
Functions and code blocks are approximately 15 lines or fewer
The example does not reference undefined external functions, types, or helpers that force the reader to mentally gap-fill; when too much scaffolding would be needed, the slice is narrowed instead
When a code block is long, the fix is moving explanation to prose rather than cutting logic
When the point being made is an ordering constraint or behavioral invariant (e.g., tombstone checked before TTL), the code demonstrates it directly rather than relying solely on prose assertion

suggested_rubric.name: Prose-Code Weight Distribution
suggested_rubric.description: Explanatory prose before code examples front-loads the conceptual model, structural choices, and failure modes so the code only needs to show the shape of the pattern. If something is explained by an inline comment, the prose hasn't done its job yet. Warnings, preconditions, and any ordering constraints that shape how the reader should interpret the code appear before the code block — not after. This specifically includes statements that describe the required evaluation order of checks (e.g., 'expiry is checked before state'), because a reader who encounters that constraint after the code has already misread the logic. When the code's structure itself is the argument — for example, the ordering of checks encodes a security invariant — prose should frame the question the code answers, not pre-explain the answer the reader should see in the code.

dimensions.label: Why the code is shaped a particular way (e.g., mutex strategy, helper functions, branching) is explained in prose before the code block
Every notable construct in the code (helpers, naming distinctions, two-phase patterns) is introduced in the preceding prose
Warnings, preconditions, and concurrency requirements appear before the code block they apply to, not after
Any statement describing the required evaluation order of checks or operations appears before the code — a reader who sees 'X is checked before Y' after the code has already misread the logic
Inline code comments do not restate what the surrounding prose has already explained

When code structure carries the argument (e.g., check ordering), prose sets up the question the code answers rather than pre-explaining the answer — letting the code teach rather than merely illustrate

suggested_rubric.name: Narrative Coherence
suggested_rubric.description: Section headers and reading order tell a coherent story — less encyclopedia entry, more 'here's what you need to know in the order you'll need it.' Headers describe what is happening at each stage, not just label the mechanism. When two sections are conceptually entangled, the one with more dangerous failure modes or that serves as a prerequisite for correct reasoning comes first.

dimensions.label: Section headers describe what is happening at each stage rather than labeling the mechanism — e.g., 'Checking Whether the Cached Token Is Still Usable' rather than 'Expiry Check'
Content that walks through a sequence of stages is broken into distinct subsections with headers, not presented as a single long block
When two sections are entangled, the one the reader needs to reason correctly about subsequent material comes first — especially state information, pitfalls, or caveats that affect interpretation
Later sections reference earlier design reasoning with explicit callbacks rather than re-explaining
When a concept depends on another section, the dependency is named with a direct reference

suggested_rubric.name: Recommendation Framing
suggested_rubric.description: When recommending an approach, the doc names the alternative pattern, acknowledges that it works, and then explains concretely why it's the worse default. Directives become reasoning the reader internalizes rather than instructions they follow blindly.

dimensions.label: The recommended approach is compared to at least one named alternative pattern
The alternative is acknowledged as functional, not dismissed outright
The specific weakness of the alternative is explained concretely
The reader finishes knowing why to use the recommendation, not just that they should
